title: pH Titration of Weak Acid vs Strong Base
aim: To determine the strength and pKa of a weak acid (Acetic acid) by titrating it against a strong base (NaOH) using a pH meter.
theory: When a weak acid is titrated with a strong base, the pH change is gradual initially, followed by a sharp increase at the equivalence point. The neutralization reaction is: CH3COOH + NaOH → CH3COONa + H2O. At the half-equivalence point, the pH of the solution is approximately equal to the pKa of the weak acid (Henderson-Hasselbalch equation). The equivalence point pH will be > 7 due to salt hydrolysis.
procedure: Calibrate the pH meter using pH 4, 7, and 9 buffer solutions.
Pipette out 10 mL of 0.1M Acetic acid into a clean 100 mL beaker.
Add enough distilled water (approx 30 mL) so the pH electrode is properly submerged.
Fill the burette with 0.1M NaOH solution and fix it to the stand.
Place the beaker on the magnetic stirrer and start stirring slowly.
Insert the pH electrode into the beaker, ensuring it doesn't touch the magnetic bead.
Record the initial pH of the acid.
Add NaOH in 0.5 mL increments from the burette and record the pH after each addition.
Near the expected equivalence point (around 8-12 mL), add NaOH in 0.1 mL increments.
Continue adding NaOH until the pH reaches around 12.
Plot pH vs Volume of NaOH and d(pH)/d(V) vs Volume to find the exact equivalence point.

requiredFormula.description: At half-equivalence point, [Salt]=[Acid], so pH = pKa.

safety: Acetic acid has a pungent smell; handle in a well-ventilated space.
NaOH is corrosive; avoid skin contact.
Handle pH electrode with extreme care; it is fragile and expensive.
Store electrode in storage solution when not in use.
